metadata.note: This database reflects commonly available and subsidised respiratory aerosol products. Subsidy status and product availability may change. Always verify current formulary status on NZF.org.nz before prescribing.

LAMA.name: Long-acting muscarinic antagonist (anticholinergic)
LAMA.description: Once- or twice-daily maintenance, slower onset (2-4 hours), sustained bronchodilation.

medicines.notes: Long-acting, 24-hour coverage. Gold standard LAMA in NZ. Takes 2-4 weeks to reach steady state.
Once-daily, rapid onset (~5 min). Alternative to tiotropium.

medicines.subsidyNote: Not subsidised; funded only if specialist-initiated
medicines.notes: Twice-daily option. Less commonly used in NZ primary care.
Available only in Anoro (with vilanterol) and Trelegy (with vilanterol + fluticasone).

LABA.name: Long-acting β₂-agonist (beta-2 agonist)
LABA.description: Once- or twice-daily maintenance bronchodilator. Faster onset (~15 min) than LAMA. Not recommended as monotherapy in COPD.

medicines.notes: Twice-daily. Older formulation; less commonly used now. Not recommended as COPD monotherapy.
Rapid onset (~5 min). Typically used in ICS/LABA combinations. Monotherapy not recommended in COPD.

medicines.nzfRef: Only in LABA/LAMA or triple combinations
medicines.notes: Ultra-LABA; only in Anoro (with umeclidinium) or Trelegy (triple).
Ultra-LABA; once-daily. Available primarily in Ultibro (with glycopyrronium).

LABA_LAMA.name: Dual therapy: LABA/LAMA combination
LABA_LAMA.description: Once-daily dual maintenance therapy. Excellent for symptomatic patients or those with exacerbation risk.

medicines.notes: Preferred dual therapy. Ultra-LABA + modern LAMA. Single inhaler, once-daily. Well-tolerated.
Alternative dual therapy. Ultra-LABA + glycopyrronium. Once-daily. Slightly higher LABA dose than Anoro.
Twice-daily option. Not commonly available in NZ primary care due to subsidy restrictions.

ICS_LABA.name: ICS/LABA combination (inhaled corticosteroid + long-acting beta-agonist)
ICS_LABA.description: Used in asthma-COPD overlap and some COPD cases (not first-line for pure COPD). Step down from triple therapy if appropriate.

medicines.nzfRef: Asthma, COPD if overlap or eosinophilia
medicines.notes: Oldest ICS/LABA. Twice-daily. Generic options available. Not preferred first-line in pure COPD.
Twice-daily. Can be used as maintenance-and-reliever therapy (MART) in asthma. Dual action inhaler.
Once-daily ICS/LABA. Can bridge patients from COPD to asthma-COPD overlap if eosinophilia emerges.

TRIPLE.name: Triple therapy: ICS/LABA/LAMA
TRIPLE.description: Three-drug combination for GOLD D (high symptoms + high exacerbation risk). Reduces exacerbations vs dual therapy.

medicines.notes: Preferred triple therapy in NZ. Single inhaler, twice-daily. ICS dose is moderate (budesonide 174 µg/day at standard dosing).

medicines.subsidyNote: Not routinely funded in NZ; specialist use only
medicines.notes: Once-daily triple. Not available for routine primary care prescribing in NZ due to cost.

SABA.name: Short-acting β₂-agonist (rescue/reliever)
SABA.description: Rapid-onset (5-15 min) PRN for breakthrough symptoms. Not for regular maintenance.

medicines.notes: Gold standard rescue inhaler. Rapid onset. No tachyphylaxis with appropriate maintenance therapy.
Less commonly used; alternative if salbutamol intolerance. Slightly longer duration (~5 hours).

SAMA.name: Short-acting muscarinic antagonist (anticholinergic)
SAMA.description: Slower onset (30-45 min) than SABA but may be useful adjunct. Can combine with SABA for synergistic effect.

medicines.frequency: Three to four times daily (regular or PRN)
medicines.notes: Can be used with SABA for additive effect (e.g., in acute exacerbations). Not preferred for regular use given LAMA availability.

OTHER.name: Other respiratory agents
OTHER.description: Useful add-ons and adjuncts for specific COPD phenotypes.

medicines.subsidyNote: Fully funded if chronic bronchitis + frequent exacerbations
medicines.notes: Phosphodiesterase-4 (PDE-4) inhibitor. Oral, not inhaled. Consider in frequent exacerbators (≥2/year) with chronic bronchitis. ~10% relative reduction in exacerbations. GI side effects common (nausea, weight loss).
For patients unable to use MDI/DPI or requiring higher drug delivery (e.g., acute exacerbations). Slower onset than MDI but higher lung deposition.

recommended.avoid: ICS as monotherapy in pure COPD

recommended.rationale: Superior to monotherapy for symptom relief and exacerbation prevention in higher-risk groups.
Reduces exacerbations vs dual therapy. Recommended only if ≥2 exac/year or high symptom burden.
recommended.ics_indication: ICS benefit seen with eosinophils ≥100 cells/µL or frequent exacerbations despite dual therapy.

ICS_cautions.note: Overuse of ICS in COPD associated with increased risk of pneumonia, oral candidiasis, and adverse metabolic effects.
guidance: Reserve ICS for GOLD D or asthma-COPD overlap.
Do not use ICS monotherapy in pure COPD.
If eosinophils <100 cells/µL and no asthma features, consider dual LABA/LAMA before triple.
Monitor for oral candidiasis; advise rinse after use.

smoking_cessation.note: Smoking cessation is the single most effective intervention. Refer to cessation service.
pharmacotherapy: Nicotine replacement therapy (patches, lozenges, gum) - available OTC or subsidised
